feed.title: SAP Community - Python
feed.subtitle: Python blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: The ABAP Detective and the Errant Electrolyte
entry.content:
An aspect of sustainability is minimal resource use, in both the sense of lower costs, but also the avoidance of energy use that contributes to carbon dioxide emissions from fossil fuels. For an establishment on "all-electric" the latter is what might be called an externality. As a code and systems analysis detective, I look for areas of improvement.

Previous investigations into environmental monitoring tools are noted at the end; this case focuses on the efficiency and use of domestic hot water delivery. In the backwaters of the united states, standard appliances include a 30 or 40 gallon (sorry: X liters) tank with a heating source constantly attempting to maintain a set minimum temperature. Other areas use the "on-demand" approach, or hot-water-as-a-service, with a heating device close to the delivery point (let's not go into the 110 versus 220 volt infrastructure as a distraction).

To the crux of the biscuit: I set up a temperature monitor directly above the output of the hot water tank, connected to a Raspberry Pi that is polled by a central system. Other approaches would be locally collecting the data and uploading in scheduled bursts, pushing rather than pulling.

TMP102 gauge, copper, insulation. (Photo by Jim Spath)

(photo by Jim Spath)

This ad hoc wiring approach works in a non-intrusive way, as the sensor unit does not touch the copper pipes, nor contact running water. Typically throughout the day, hot water is not running, so the readings are close to ambient room temperature. When a tap is opened somewhere, preheated water goes near enough to tick up the temperature reading, then after the flows stops the temperature gradually degrades. Or, if the tank completely empties of heated water, the readings will correspondingly decline.

Standalone sensor:

TMP102 gauge with blips

(screenshot from Zabbix web client)

A few times during the day, temperature rises, then declines. Not a particularly useful data set if you want to ask questions like "how hot is the hot water" or "how long did the washer run?" or even "how much did this unit of work cost?". The human brain can glance at these shapes and discern patterns, like Sherlock Holmes checking out cigar-ash-flakes in a pursuit. Writing an algorithm is a bit trickier.

The Zabbix open source suite I'm using has tools to run calculations on data feeds; for instance in some cases I'd pull values in Celsius, then add a derived parameter converting to Fahrenheit. There are ways to "look back" to prior values to determine deltas (which I've not implemented yet). You can also set up triggers to perform actions such as alerting on worst case scenarios (like overheating or frozen pipes: Ow!).

I'll gloss over the gnarly details of the Sparkfun/QWIIC monitoring hardware/software and cut to the chase: only one specific gauge per Pi, without adding multiplexing I2C complexities, so I was faced with deploying 2 Pi's with the same gauge type, or add a different gauge on 1 Pi. I chose the latter. Then the case went into what we colloquially called a "mung" state. Not "hung" but not right either.

Pi and the OS

If I could, I'd "flame" here, showing the rapid pace of software development breaks things that worked a few short years ago in surprising ways. I'm using code that someone else wrote in bash shell scripting back in 2012 or so, and that logic doesn't need to be tweaked when a new Linux kernel or even a subsystem component is written. And generally, Python examples the vendors share keeps working from year to year. Upgrades to the running environment are non-breaking other than minor steps like "oh, use Python 3.12 now instead of 3.10" that are easy enough to resolve.

My first Pi platform had a "6.1" 32-bit Linux kernel version; more recently new installs have a "6.6" kernel, where 64-bit flavors are as stable as the 32-bit. Great, why would that matter? Somewhere in the matrix, Python grew an appendage, nicknamed "venv" meaning a virtual environment. Let's add containers, crates, and virtualization while we're at it. Except this is a $10 machine with a single purpose.

TMP102 and BME280

These 2 temperature sensors are available in the chosen "ecosystem" that connects the Pi brain to the digital sensors, which could also include humidity and barometric pressure. Since I had collected and deployed at least one of each, I assumed connecting both to 1 Pi would be simple. I had code samples in shell and Python for both. "Just" follow the manufacturers instructions and "pip" it into place (no, not the old DEC PIP peripheral to peripheral connector, the new python software package installer). Failure to launch:

 

$ pip install qwiic_bme280
error: externally-managed-environment

 

Oh, darn, this didn't work, and spit out a bunch of scary messages, especially this last dooms-day-scenario:

 

You can override this, at the risk of breaking \
 your Python installation or OS,  \
 by passing --break-system-packages. \
hint: See PEP 668 for the detailed specification.

 

"Break system packages" sounds ominous. Maybe don't take that approach. 

pip breakage

(screenshot from terminal session)

A more staid detective would go read the reference manuals, consult others online, re-read the source code, then diligently make necessary repairs. Others more reactive might literally check with their vendors and ask what's up with this. Investigators with a hacking tendency would start typing likely fixes and rout/route around the damage. Command line use of a venv turned out to be fairly simple:

 

python3 -m venv venv
venv/bin/pip3 install sparkfun-qwiic-bme280

[...]
Successfully installed \
 smbus2-0.5.0 \
 sparkfun-qwiic-bme280-0.9.0 \
 sparkfun-qwiic-i2c-1.0.0

 

No doubt more experienced Python developers are aghast at the hammer approach I used.

Getting readings at the command line would now work with 2 sensors; the new one shows:

 

$ venv/bin/python3.11  /path/to/sparkfun.py  temperature_celsius
16.2118

 

Fine, a 3-pipe problem rather than a trivial 2-pipe one. Except the whole intent is to automate sensor data collection, not have to run a command line manually. I'll leave out the messy trial-and-error details, other than to share that the Zabbix process debug steps boil down to seeing whether a parameter shows as "not supported" or returns an error message such as "got string, expecting a number" in the console, and checking the agent logs for clues (like: "The Qwiic BME280 device isn't connected to the system. Please check your connection" when I used the wrong sensor).

Stuffed inside an old shell script are new links to the monitor logic.

 

/path/venv/python3 /path/sparkfun-venv.py temperature_celsius

 

The middle section didn't need to have any virtual environment goo but for the sake of my future self looking at these sensors after the next incompatibility will possibly recall why.

Before going to the trouble of writing calculation or trigger steps in parameter definitions I used a handy UI feature in Zabbix that overlays 2 measurements in one chart. First, make sure both metrics are stable:

2 charts

(screenshot from Zabbix web client)

Then, add the second parameter to a dashboard widget. I didn't muck around with colors, lines, or transparencies, taking the defaults as good enough for the quick quality control / QC check.

2 temperature sensors on 1 chart

(screenshot from Zabbix web client)

Next problems to solve?

  • The 2 gauges differ under "no flow" states by over 1 degree.
  • Alerts/triggers
  • Validation/calibration

Calibration

Back in the last century, my polytechnic/polymath education included calibrating thermometers by determining how close their readings were to freezing boiling water temperatures. A memorable lesson in theory versus practice, tossing aside the novice assumption that a device always shows accurate values. How many degrees or tenths-of-degrees plus or minus the correct reading? (let's skip over side issues such as does water boil at exactly 100 degrees Celsius at any specific elevation or location).

What I'd probably investigate next is recording the temperature at the taps compared to the utility room infrastructure, particularly after hearing anecdotal comments such as "the kitchen sink faucet is not hot enough."

NOTES / REFERENCES

Shell:

  • https://znil.net/index.php/Zabbix:Template_Raspberry_Pi
  • https://znil.net/index.php?title=Temperatur_/_Spannung_etc._des_Raspberry_Pi_selbst_auslesen

Python:

  • http://rptl.io/venv
  • https://docs.python.org/3/library/venv.html
  • https://peps.python.org/pep-0668/
  • https://github.com/sparkfun/qwiic_bme280_py

SAP Community posts:

  • The ABAP Detective Takes The Summer Heat
  • The ABAP Detective Gets Their Clock Cleaned
  • The ABAP Detective Takes The Heat, or How To Cleanse Data

Also:

  • Intra home detectors

entry.title: Hands-on Tutorial: Creating an FAQ assistant as tool for a BTP AI agent
entry.content:
If you have a collection of FAQs that you want to be easily accessible for your business users, then an assistant that is powered by a Large Language model might be part of the answer.

This blog explains how to create such a backend (REST-API), that could directly feed into a graphical chatbot, or that can even be used as tool in a more comprehensive AI agent. In case this introduction sounds somehow familiar, then you might have read an earlier blog, which evolved into this tutorial. New in this blog is the use of SAP HANA Cloud's embedding engine for text. Also, a separate blog will explain how to bring this FAQ logic into an AI agent running on the SAP Business Technology Platform.

Table of contents

  • Background
  • Architecture and Process Flows
  • Prerequisites
  • The Frequently Asked Questions
  • Obtaining the "best" answer to a user request
  • Deployment as REST-API on Cloud Foundry

You find all code of this tutorial in this repository (Jupyter Notebooks + Cloud Foundry application).

Background

A collection of Frequently Asked Questions can be a great help to deal with common requests for specific information. However, the longer the list, the harder it can be to find the one piece of information one is looking for. Having to scroll through a long list can be tedious, and a simple text search might miss the one item you are looking for.

Hence an FAQ assistant can be very useful, especially if it can deal with user questions, that are phrased differently to the curated list of Questions and Answers.

Maybe you are rolling out new software to your users (S/4?) and want to help them along finding their feet in the new system through an FAQ assistant. Or you have a list of FAQs for any other purpose, whether intended for internal colleagues or external contacts such as customers. A digital assistant that leverages the list of FAQs could help the users along.

In this blog you see how the backbone of such a digital assistant can be created, based on a fairly short list of FAQs about SAP and working at SAP. Whilst this example is quite simplified, the same overall approach has been working very well for a customer with a list of 200+ FAQs. Maybe you find some inspiration in this blog for your own project.

Architecture and Process Flows

Before looking at any code, let's first get an understanding of how the application works at high level.

The architecture and process flows are based on the requirement, that the assistant should not hallucinate. As exciting as Large Language Models are, when producing text they are producing text that seems likely to them, but the text might be simply made up and incorrect.

To ensure that the chatbot can become a trusted advisor, hallucinations need to be avoided. We achieve this, by using the Large Language Model to understand the user's request, to find the predefined Question from the existing FAQ that best matches that request. It's then easy to find the corresponding pre-defined answer for that question. The only text generation happens now, when the pre-defined answer is reworded to be a natural response to the incoming request. This introduces some risk, that the re-wording might introduce a mistake. However, customers seem to prefer the natural wording of the response, accepting a (I like to think small) risk of producing factually incorrect output. If you prefer, you can drop this last step of course and return the exact response from the knowledge pool, thereby avoiding any risk of a hallucination.

The overall process flow for an incoming request is:

  1. An incoming request is received, ie "What is the meaning of the letters SAP".
  2. This text is vectorised by SAP HANA Cloud's vector engine. The same engine finds the most similar pre-defined questions from its known collection of FAQs.
  3. The original incoming request and the reduced list of candidate questions (as determined by the vector engine in the previous step) are sent via the Generative AI Hub to a Large Language Model, with the prompt to identify which question is the best match. The model returns the ID of the single, most relevant question.
  4. Now that the best matching question from the predefined list of FAQs is known, the predefined answer that belongs to the predefined question from the FAQ is retrieved from SAP HANA Cloud.
  5. The Large Language Model is used again, now to adjust the working of the pre-defined answer to the wording of the incoming request. This step helps create a more natural conversation flow.
  6. This adjusted answer is returned as response to the incoming request. 

 

Administrators can upload Questions and Answers with this simple process flow:

  1. Upload the new Question and Answer to SAP HANA Cloud
  2. SAP HANA Cloud automatically creates the embedding of the question and stores the vector in the same table that holds the actual texts. We will see later how this is done.

Questions and Answers are stored in two separate tables. This allows for 1:n relationships between Answers and Question. This means, for each predefined Answer 1 or more Questions can be associated. This will be useful when improving and adjusting the assistant to different terminology from the different users. After all, there are many ways to phrase the same question.

The overall underlying Architecture is:

Since SAP HANA Cloud's embedding engine and the SAP-hosted mistral-large-instruct model are used, no externally hosted model needs to be called. All processing is happening on the BTP.

Prerequisites

To follow the implementation hands-on, you require these components:

  • SAP Generative AI Hub with the model "mistral-large-instruct" deployed,
  • SAP HANA Cloud
    - to store the questions and answers
    - to create the embeddings of the questions with the vector engine
    - to compare questions with the vector engine
  • Python environment, ie Jupyter Notebooks in Miniconda,  for sandboxing and testing
  • Cloud Foundry on BTP if you want to deploy the application as REST-API

This blog assumes that you already have some familiarity with Python and Jupyter Notebooks. However, this project could also be a starting point to become familiar with those components. Personally, I like Miniconda to create a local Python environment and local Jupyter Notebooks. The blog "Machine Learning with SAP HANA Cloud" that was co-written with @YannickSchaper gives an overview of how to get started with our Python package hana_ml. That hana_ml package allows Data Scientists to work from Python with data that remains in SAP HANA Cloud (or SAP Datasphere). It can even trigger Machine Learning in SAP HANA Cloud, but we will use it here mostly to upload data, enrich the data and to trigger the vector engine.

The Frequently Asked Questions

We will use a fairly short list of FAQs as basis for the Chatbot. These are just a few examples taken from the FAQs about SAP overall (ie history and sustainability) and Jobs @ SAP. Kudos to who knows by heart what the abbreviation "SAP" actually stand for...  For all others there is the FAQ and our little custom assistant we are building.

The Questions and Answers for our chatbot are saved in two separate Excel files. This allows for specifying multiple Questions that belong to the same single Answer. Remember, all files and code used in this blog can be downloaded from this repository.  The code to upload the FAQs is in 010 Upload Questions and Answers.ipynb. To run the code yourself you should just have to enter your own logon credentials for SAP HANA Cloud and the Generative AI Hub into the file credentials.json. In this blog itself I am only showing the most important pieces of code. Please check the above repository to get the full code. To be able to execute the Notebooks you also need to install these Python packages

  • !pip install hana_ml
  • !pip install openpyxl
  • !pip install "generative-ai-hub-sdk[all]"

Uploading the data to SAP HANA Cloud is then easy with the hana_ml Python package. Establish a connection from Python to SAP HANA Cloud.

import hana_ml.dataframe as dataframe
conn = dataframe.ConnectionContext(
                                   address  = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_ADDRESS,
                                   port     = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PORT,
                                   user     = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_USER,
                                   password = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PASSWORD, 
                                  )
conn.connection.isconnected()

Load the questions from Excel (FAQ_ASSISTANT_ANSWERS.xlsx) for into a Pandas DataFrame.

#!pip install openpyxl
import pandas as pd
df_data= pd.read_excel ('FAQ_ASSISTANT_QUESTIONS.xlsx') 
df_data.head(5)

And upload that Pandas DataFrame to SAP HANA Cloud. The table FAQ_ASSISTANT_QUESTIONS will be created automatically. Now add to that new table a vector column, that will be automatically populated with the text embeddings of the QUESTION column.

dbapi_cursor = conn.connection.cursor()
dbapi_cursor.execute("""
ALTER TABLE FAQ_ASSISTANT_QUESTIONS
ADD (QUESTION_VECTOR REAL_VECTOR GENERATED ALWAYS AS VECTOR_EMBEDDING(QUESTION, 'QUERY', 'SAP_NEB.20240715'));
""")

Verify that SAP HANA Cloud's embedding engine has created the vectors as expected. 

conn.table('FAQ_ASSISTANT_QUESTIONS').head(5).collect()

You can now add additional rows to the table as usual and SAP HANA Cloud's embedding engine will automatically kick in to fill the vector column. The Notebook 010 Upload Questions and Answers.ipynb has an example that shows this in action.

Similarly, upload the pre-defined answers from FAQ_ASSISTANT_ANSWERS.xlsx. These texts do not need to be vectorised, as we are only comparing the questions with each other.

df_data = pd.read_excel ('FAQ_ASSISTANT_ANSWERS.xlsx') 
df_remote = dataframe.create_dataframe_from_pandas(connection_context=conn, 
                                                   pandas_df=df_data, 
                                                   table_name='FAQ_ASSISTANT_ANSWERS',
                                                   force=True,
                                                   replace=False)

Obtaining the "best" answer to a user request

Now let's play through a scenario of a user asking a question, which the application is trying to answer. This section's code is also in 020 Ask a Question.ipynb. Take a first question and have SAP HANA Cloud vectorise it.

user_question = 'What is the meaning of the letters SAP?'
sql = f'''SELECT VECTOR_EMBEDDING('{user_question}', 'QUERY', 'SAP_NEB.20240715') EMBEDDEDQUESTION FROM DUMMY;'''
user_question_embedding_str = conn.sql(sql).head(1).collect().iloc[0, 0]
user_question_embedding_str[:10]

This vectorisation is required so that the user question can be compared with the existing vectors of the known questions. This finds the most similar known questions.

sql = f'''SELECT TOP 200 "AID", "QID", "QUESTION", COSINE_SIMILARITY("QUESTION_VECTOR", TO_REAL_VECTOR('{user_question_embedding_str}')) AS SIMILARITY
        FROM FAQ_ASSISTANT_QUESTIONS ORDER BY "SIMILARITY" DESC, "AID", "QID" '''
df_remote = conn.sql(sql)
df_remote.head(20).collect()

Take the most promising questions and format that collection into a text element, which will become part of the prompt that is sent to the Large Language  Model to select the best match. We will see that each question is preceded by an ID, which is a combination of the QID and AID. This new composite ID will help us to retrieve the corresponding data from the tables in SAP HANA Cloud.

top_n = max(df_remote.filter('SIMILARITY > 0.95').count(), 10)
df_data = df_remote.head(top_n).select('AID', 'QID', 'QUESTION').collect()
df_data['ROWID'] = df_data['AID'].astype(str) + '-' + df_data['QID'].astype(str) + ': '
df_data = df_data[['ROWID', 'QUESTION']]
candiates_str = df_data.to_string(header=False,
                                  index=False,
                                  index_names=False)

Leverage that information in the prompt for the Large Language Model, by describing the task.

llm_prompt = f'''
Task: which of the following candidate questions is closest to this one?
{user_question}
Only return the ID of the selected question, not the question itself. The ID are the first values in each row. 
In case none of the candidate questions is a good match, return only: NONE

-----------------------------------

Candidate questions. Each question starts with the ID, followed by a :, followed by the question
{candiates_str}
'''
print(llm_prompt)

Specify which Large Language Model should be used on the Generative AI Hub (this model needs to be deployed there) and send the prompt off.

AI_CORE_MODEL_NAME  = 'mistralai--mistral-large-instruct'
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.openai import chat
messages = [{"role": "user", "content": llm_prompt}
           ]
kwargs = dict(model_name=AI_CORE_MODEL_NAME, messages=messages, temperature=0)
response = chat.completions.create(**kwargs)
llm_response = response.choices[0].message.content.lstrip()
llm_response

The model responds '1001-1', which refers to the first Question 1 of Answer 1001. And indeed, that's the correct match. Now get the answer to that question from SAP HANA Cloud.

aid = qid = None
matching_question = matching_answer = None

if llm_response != 'NONE':
   aid, qid = llm_response.split('-')

   # From HANA Cloud get the question from the FAQ that matches the user request best
   df_remote = conn.table('FAQ_ASSISTANT_QUESTIONS').filter(f''' "AID" = '{aid}' AND "QID" = '{qid}' ''').select('QUESTION')
   matching_question = df_remote.head(5).collect().iloc[0,0]
    
   # From HANA Cloud get the predefined answer of the above question from the FAQ
   df_remote = conn.table('FAQ_ASSISTANT_ANSWERS').filter(f''' "AID" = '{aid}' ''').select('ANSWER')
   matching_answer = df_remote.head(5).collect().iloc[0,0]
matching_answer

The mystery of what the letters SAP stand for has been solved. Now, optionally, you can tweak this pre-defined answer to be a more natural response to the initial incoming question. Let's ask the Large Language Model again.

llm_prompt = f'''
Task: Answer the following question. Only consider the context that is provided further below.
{user_question}

-----------------------------------

Context to consider to answer the above question:
{matching_answer}
'''
messages = [{"role": "user", "content": llm_prompt}           ]
kwargs = dict(model_name=AI_CORE_MODEL_NAME, messages=messages, temperature=0)
response = chat.completions.create(**kwargs)
natural_answer = response.choices[0].message.content.lstrip()
print(f"""
The user question was:
{user_question}

The selected question from the FAQ is:
{matching_question}

The pre-defined answer to the selected question is:
{matching_answer}

The natural answer is:
{natural_answer}""")

Indeed, the natural answer picks up some of the initial question's terminology and feels like a more natural response.

 

Deployment as REST-API on Cloud Foundry

Above we have the application logic. Now we want to make it available as REST-API to a custom BTP AI Agent. That AI agent will be described in a separate Tutorial that will hopefully be published soon...

For now we create the REST-API on Cloud Foundry. In case you are new to Cloud Foundry, then Steps 1 and 2 of this Learning Journey get you started. The application that we deploy here consists of 4 files. The full code is available in this repository folder.  

  1. faq.py ➔ holds the actual application logic
  2. manifest.yaml ➔ contains for instance the memory requirements and environment variables
  3. requirements.txt ➔ lists the Python packages that will be installed
  4. runtime.txt ➔ specifies the Python version (python-3.12.x)

Before pushing the application to Cloud Foundry you need to make the following adjustments

  1. In faq.py specify your SAP HANA Cloud logon credentials. For productive use you may want to store these more securely, ie in the SAP Credential Store. An example is in this blog. 
  2. In manifest.yml enter your credentials for the SAP Generative AI Hub.

Then push your application to Cloud Foundry.

cf push

And you should see the URL of your REST-API!

 

Test the URL from your preferred environment. This could just be the Python notebook.

import requests
paylod = {'user_request': 'Who is the boss at SAP?'}
url = 'https://REPLACEWITHYOURURL'
headers = {'Accept' : 'application/json', 'Content-Type' : 'application/json'}
r = requests.get(url, json=paylod, headers=headers, verify=False)
response = r.json()
faq_response = response['faq_response'] # final answer
faq_response_log = response['faq_response_log'] # log of the individual steps

And it worked! We get the official answer as written on SAP's own FAQ, adjusted to a more natural conversation flow.

Now you might think, wouldn't it be nice if the assistant was getting the ultimate answer (the name of the CEO) from the answer's link? That's where the BTP AI agent comes in. You can continue right here with the next hands-on tutorial "Creating a custom AI agent on the BTP".

entry.title: SAP CodeJam HANA ML in India 2025-03 Recap
entry.content:
At the beginning of March 2025, as part of the ML/AI Tour my fellow dev advocate @noravonthenen and I did in India, we held five SAP CodeJam events in Kolkata, Pune, and Bengaluru.

Two of these events focused on getting started with Machine Learning using SAP HANA and Python.

Equally important were chances to catch up with some organizers and participants in informal post-work settings...

...or with our SAP colleagues.

I am glad Nora and I could help so many participants to go through the journey of improving their skills working with ML and AI!

Traditionally, I very much enjoyed SAP Stammtisch at the end of the last CodeJam of the Tour.

#IamSAP 

I always take the opportunity to experience at least a little bit of the city I am in, so you can see my photos:

  • Kolkata: https://www.instagram.com/p/DG1wL0OydCp/ 
  • Pune: https://www.instagram.com/p/DG75rf0MPkt/ 

Now, the next stop for me is in Kraków on April 3rd.

Would you like to host such an SAP CodeJam? Here are the details: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/quot-getting-started-with-machine-learning-usin...

entry.title: Hands-on Tutorial: Creating a custom AI agent on the BTP
entry.content:
SAP is going full speed with AI agents on Joule. Indeed, it's incredible how such agents can address complex requests by orchestrating a set of generic tools they are given access to.

If you have a need to build your own custom AI agent on BTP, then this tutorial will hopefully be a good start.

  • Is this tutorial production-ready? No
  • Does it provide inspiration and a head start for your own projects? I would like to think so
  • Can you learn how AI agents work and how to create and extend your own? Absolutely! You will see how to create an AI agent using the open source framework LangGraph on the BTP.

Table of contents

  • Background
  • Architecture 
  • Prerequisites
  • Develop an AI Agent in a Jupyter Notebook
  • Deploy the AI Agent as REST-API
  • Put a sandbox user interface on top

You find all code of this tutorial in this repository.
And thanks go to @CanAbdulla for his early feedback on the blog before it went live.

 

Background

Let's take a common business requirement as task for creating a custom BTP AI agent.

Send an email to Peter and let him know whether invoice XYZ been paid.

In the ideal world you would leverage SAP standard functionality, ie Joule. If that's not possible for whatever reason, we can use BTP to make our own agent. To make it easy to implement this example, the AI agent is not actually connecting to a live ERP system. Instead the response of the invoice status will be faked. However, the AI agent doesn't know that the invoice status is made up and it should not be too tricky to connect the agent to a SAP system.

Instead of hardcoding the workflow of getting the invoice status and sending the email, we provide the AI agent with specific tools (here Python functions) that it can decide on it's own how to use them and in which sequence. These function can take one or more parameters as input and return a response themselves:

  • get_invoice_status(invoice_id)
  • get_email_address(name)
  • send_email(recipient_name, email_address, email_text)

An AI agent shines best when these tools can be used for a number of different purposes. Hence the AI agent is also given these additional tools for you to be creative with:

  • answer_SAP_question(text): Delegates questions about SAP to an FAQ assistant
  • get_text_from_link(link): Retrieves the text from a website. You will see later how this tool is useful in combination with the above FAQ assistant
  • get_lunch_menu(): Gets today's menu from the SAP canteen in Zurich, Switzerland, as example of website scraping. That website is in German only, but you can test whether the AI agent can translate the text to English, or whether this could be an additional tool for the AI agent.
  • get_live_tv_arte(): Obtain a description of what's currently shown on the French/German TV station ARTE, as an example of calling a REST-API. In case you are outside France or Germany you might not be able to see the live stream, but the description of what's on should be visible.

 

Architecture

The architecture of the AI agent can be very lean. At a minimum you just need:

  • SAP Generative AI Hub: To access Claude, the Large Language Model which is powering this AI agent
  • Cloud Foundry: The code of the actual AI agent is deployed as a REST-API on Cloud Foundry. This tutorial also includes a quick sandboxing user interface that is also deployed on Cloud Foundry.

Only in case you want to implement the FAQ assistant that answers questions about SAP, you also need:

  • SAP HANA Cloud: To store the texts and to create, store and compare the text's embeddings
  • Mistral-Large-Instruct: A Large Language Model, that is hosted by SAP for text processing and creation

 

Prerequisites

To follow the implementation hands-on, you require these components:

  • SAP Generative AI Hub with the model "claude-3.5-sonnet" deployed,
  • Python environment, ie Jupyter Notebooks in Miniconda,  for sandboxing and testing
  • Cloud Foundry on BTP if you want to deploy the application as REST-API

Optionally: For the AI assistant to be able to answer questions about SAP, you also need to implement the tutorial Creating an FAQ assistant as tool for a BTP AI agent. 

This blog assumes that you already have some familiarity with Python and Jupyter Notebooks. However, this project could also be a starting point to become familiar with those components. Personally, I like Miniconda to create a local Python environment and local Jupyter Notebooks. The blog "Machine Learning with SAP HANA Cloud" that was co-written with @YannickSchaper gives an overview of setting up a miniconda environment.

You need to install these packages in your Notebook environment

  • !pip install langchain_core
  • !pip install langgraph
  • !pip install "generative-ai-hub-sdk[all]"

Develop an AI Agent in a Jupyter Notebook

All code for the BTP AI agent can be downloaded from this repository. First things first. In the file credentials.json enter your own logon details for SAP AI Core and your own SMTP email server. Now run the code from 010 BTP AI agent.ipynb. Load these credentials and apply them.

import json, os
with open('./credentials.json', 'r') as creds:
  credentials = json.load(creds)
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_ID"]      = credentials["SAP_AI_CORE"]["AICORE_CLIENT_ID"]
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"]  = credentials["SAP_AI_CORE"]["AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"]
os.environ["AICORE_AUTH_URL"]       = credentials["SAP_AI_CORE"]["AICORE_AUTH_URL"]
os.environ["AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"] = credentials["SAP_AI_CORE"]["AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"]
os.environ["AICORE_BASE_URL"]       = credentials["SAP_AI_CORE"]["AICORE_BASE_URL"]   
smtp_server   = credentials["SMTP"]["SMTP_SERVER"]
smtp_port     = credentials["SMTP"]["SMTP_PORT"]
smtp_user     = credentials["SMTP"]["SMTP_USER"]
smtp_password = credentials["SMTP"]["SMTP_PASSWORD"]

Now we start with the AI agent. The following functions will become tools for the agent. Each function starts with a comment of what the function does and which input parameters it requires. Those comments are useful for us humans, but they are also picked up by the agent to understand what each function does, which input it requires and what it returns. That's already sufficient for the agent to decide whether and  how to use each tool/function.

To make the code work you will just need to make these adjustments:

  • Enter your own email addresses in the get_email_address() function
  • Enter the URL of your FAQ assistant in the answer_SAP_question() function. This is optional, you can also decide not to use this function.

 

Get invoice status
import random
def get_invoice_status(invoice_id: str) -> str:
   """Returns an invoice's status, ie whether it has been paid or not.

   Args:
      invoice_id: The invoice id
   """

   # This function mocks retrieving the invoice status from a live system
   # See SAP's API documentation for the real / live API that can provide this information from your system, ie the InvoiceClearingStatus on
   # https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/19d48293097f4a2589433856b034dfa5/cb3caf09bd6749c59f0765981032b74e.html?locale=en-US
   options = ['Paid', 'Overdue', 'Unpaid, not due yet']
   invoice_status = random.choice(options)
   response = f"The status of invoice {invoice_id} is: {invoice_status}." 
    
   return response

 

Get email address
def get_email_address(name: str) -> str:
   """Returns the person's email address

   Args:
      name: The name of the person whose email address is returned
   """

   # This function mocks retrieving an email address from a live system
   dict = {}
   dict['Ewald'] = 'enteryourownemail@yourcompany.com.xyz'
   dict['Stefan'] = 'enteryourownemail@yourcompany.com.xyz'
   dict['Fabian'] = 'enteryourownemail@yourcompany.com.xyz'

   if name in dict.keys():
      response = dict[name]
   else:
      response = dict['Ewald']
   return response

 

Send email
import smtplib, ssl
from email.mime.text import MIMEText
def send_email(recipient_name: str, email_address: str, email_text: str) -> str:
   """Sends emails. Returns a status update.

   Args:
      recipient_name: The name of the email recipient
      email_address: The recipient's email address
      email_text: The email's text that will be send
   """
    
   # This function uses SMTP credentials to send an email directly from Python
   # For productive use you may want to leverage a BTP component, for instance the Alert Notification service
   # https://help.sap.com/docs/alert-notification/sap-alert-notification-for-sap-btp/email-action-type?locale=en-US
    
   # Prepare email content
   subject = 'Email from your SAP BTP AI Agent'
   content = email_text
   msg = MIMEText(content, 'plain')
   msg['Subject'] =  subject
   msg['From']   = smtp_user 

   # Send the email
   context = ssl.create_default_context()  
   with smtplib.SMTP_SSL(smtp_server, smtp_port, context=context) as server:
      server.login(smtp_user, smtp_password)
      server.sendmail(from_addr=smtp_user, to_addrs=email_address, msg=msg.as_string())

   return f"I sent the email to {recipient_name} ({email_address}): \n{email_text}"

 

Answer SAP question
### TODO Requires the implementation of the FAQ assistant as described in:
### https://community.sap.com/t5/artificial-intelligence-and-machine-learning-blogs/hands-on-tutorial-creating-an-faq-assistant-as-tool-for-a-btp-ai-agent/ba-p/14027300

import requests
def answer_SAP_question(text: str) -> str:
   """Responds to questions about the company SAP

   Args:
      text: The question about SAP
   """

   # Forward the incoming question to the AI assistant's REST-API
   backend_api = "https://ENTERYOURURLFROMTHEAIASSISTANT/"
   user_input = text
   paylod = {'user_request': user_input}
   headers = {'Accept' : 'application/json', 'Content-Type' : 'application/json'}
   r = requests.get(backend_api, json=paylod, headers=headers, verify=False)
   response = r.json()

   # Obtain the response and response log
   faq_response = response['faq_response']
   faq_response_log = response['faq_response_log']

   # Return the response    
   return faq_response

 

Get text from link

To attempt to retrieve the actual answer if the FAQ assistant's answer point to website.

import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup
def get_text_from_link(link: str) -> str:
   """Returns the text of the given website link

   Args:
      link: The link of the webiste whose text is to be returned
   """
    
   # Header to get automated access to SAP's website content
   headers = {
      'referer': 'https://www.scrapingcourse.com/ecommerce/',
      'accept-language': 'en-US,en;q=0.9',
      'content-type': 'application/json',
      'accept-encoding': 'gzip, deflate, br',
      'sec-ch-device-memory': '8',
      'sec-ch-ua': '"Google Chrome";v="125", "Chromium";v="125", "Not.A/Brand";v="24"',
      'sec-ch-ua-platform': "Windows",
      'sec-ch-ua-platform-version': '"10.0.0"',
      'sec-ch-viewport-width': '792'
   }
   # Obtain the website content
   response = requests.get(link, headers=headers)

   # Simple parsing to get the website's text
   soup = BeautifulSoup(response.content, 'html.parser')
   link_text = soup.body.get_text()
   link_text = link_text.replace('\n', '')
   link_text = link_text.replace('Javascript must be enabled for the correct page display', '')

   return link_text

 

Get lunch menu

An example for webscraping in an AI agent tool. Update 15 April 2025: The canteen's website changed and the following scraping logic doesn't work anymore. The agent will not throw an error, but it cannot retrieve today's lunch menu anymore.

import requests
from datetime import datetime
def get_lunch_menu() -> str:
   """Returns today's menu of the SAP canteen in Zurich
   """
    
   # Scrape the canteen's website
   response = requests.get("https://circle.sv-restaurant.ch/de/menuplan/chreis-14/")
   soup = BeautifulSoup(response.content, 'html.parser')
    
   # Get current day of week
   dt = datetime.now()
   weekday_current = dt.weekday()
    
   # If called on weekend, use Monday instead
   if weekday_current < 5:
       weekday_menu = weekday_current
   else:
       weekday_menu = 0
    
   # Get date for which menu will be returned
   dates_raw = soup.find_all(class_='date')
   dates = []
   for day in dates_raw:
       dates.append(day.text)
   date = dates[0] # Past dates are removed from the restaurant page
    
   # Get menus for that date
   menus = []
   menus_raw = dates_raw = soup.find_all(id='menu-plan-tab' + str(weekday_menu))
   menus_all_raw = soup.find(id='menu-plan-tab1')
   menus_all = menus_all_raw.find_all(class_='menu-title')
   for menu in menus_all:
       if menu.text not in ['Lunch auf der Terrasse']:
           menus.append(menu.text)
           
   # Prepare the response with the above information
   menu_flowtext = ''
   for i in range(len(menus)):
       menu_flowtext += " " + str(i+1) + ") " + menus[i]
   menu_flowtext = menu_flowtext.lstrip()    
   response = f"On {weekday_menu}, the {date}, Chreis 14 serves {menu_flowtext}."

   return response

 

Get current program on TV station ARTE

An example for calling a REST-API from an AI agent tool.

import requests
def get_live_tv_arte() -> str:
   """Returns what is currently shown on the TV stastion ARTE
   """
    
   response = requests.get('https://api.arte.tv/api/player/v2/config/de/LIVE')
   data = response.json()
   title = data['data']['attributes']['metadata']['title']
   description = data['data']['attributes']['metadata']['description']

   return title + ': ' + description

 

Now collect the names of these tool functions in a list. This list will make those functions accessible to the AI agent later on. In case you haven't implemented the AI assistant REST-API you need to remove "answer_SAP_question" from the list.

tools = [get_invoice_status, get_email_address, send_email, answer_SAP_question, get_text_from_link, get_lunch_menu, get_live_tv_arte]

 

The AI agent

We are ready for the best part, let's create the AI agent. Get the imports out of the way.

from langchain_core.messages import SystemMessage
from langgraph.graph import START, StateGraph, MessagesState
from langgraph.prebuilt import tools_condition, ToolNode
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.init_models import init_llm
import urllib3
urllib3.disable_warnings()

 

Initialise the Large Language Model and provide it with the list of tools / functions.

llm = init_llm('anthropic--claude-3.5-sonnet', max_tokens=300)
llm_with_tools = llm.bind_tools(tools)

 

Configure the AI agent's behaviour with a system message.

sys_msg = SystemMessage(content="You are a helfpul assistant tasked with answering questions about different topics. Your name is 'SAP BTP AI Agent'. Keep your answers short. After giving a response, do not ask for additional requests. Instead of referring to a link on your response call the function get_text_from_link to get the information from a given link yourself. Only use information that is provided to you by the different tools you are given. When sending email include a greeting and a salutation.")

 

Define the Assistant node, which leverages the Large Language model, the bound tools and the system message as basis of the AI agent.

def assistant(state: MessagesState):
   return {"messages": [llm_with_tools.invoke([sys_msg] + state["messages"])]}

 

Build and compile the graph, which defines the AI agent's capabilities. For full flexibility all tools are connected directly to the initial assistant node. Alternatively tools could be specifically assigned and dedicated as subcomponents to other tools.

builder = StateGraph(MessagesState)
builder.add_node("assistant", assistant)
builder.add_node("tools", ToolNode(tools))
builder.add_edge(START, "assistant")
builder.add_conditional_edges(
   "assistant",
   # If the latest message (result) from assistant is a tool call -> tools_condition routes to Tools
   # If the latest message (result) from assistant is not a tool call -> tools_condition routes to END
   tools_condition,
)
builder.add_edge("tools", "assistant")
graph = builder.compile()

 

The Graph is built and can be visualised. There isn't much too see, since there is a Start node, followed by the assistant, to which all tools are connected. And there is an end. After all the code it's just good to have a visual...

 

Now test the AI agent! Start easy with something simple. Has a specific invoice been paid?

user_input = "Has invoice 42 been paid?" 
agent_outcome = graph.invoke({"messages": [("user", user_input)]})
print(agent_outcome['messages'][-1].to_json()['kwargs']['content'])

 

And the answer in this case is:
"Yes, invoice 42 has been paid. The status of the invoice is confirmed as "Paid" according to our records."

How did the agent come to that conclusion? Pull the most important information from the AI agent's log.

messages_extract = []
for msg in agent_outcome['messages']:
    msg_actor = type(msg).__name__
    msg_text = msg.content
    if msg_actor == 'AIMessage':
        if msg_text == '':
           msg_text = msg.tool_calls
    if msg_actor == 'ToolMessage':
        msg_actor = msg_actor + ' (' + msg.name + ')'
    messages_extract.append([msg_actor, msg_text])
print(str(messages_extract).replace('],', '],\n'))

[['HumanMessage', 'Has invoice 42 been paid?'],
['AIMessage', [{'name': 'get_invoice_status', 'args': {'invoice_id': '42'}, 'id': 'toolu_bdrk_01S8LnoAEMaXo2e7VStSkdA5', 'type': 'tool_call'}]],
['ToolMessage (get_invoice_status)', 'The status of invoice 42 is: Paid.'],
['AIMessage', 'Yes, invoice 42 has been paid. The status of the invoice is confirmed as "Paid" according to our records.']]

The color coding helps follow the logic.

  • The execution starts the HumanMessage. That's us.
  • The next row starting with AIMessage shows that the AI agent decided to call the get_invoice_status function, passing the invoice_id 42 as parameter.
  • The ToolMessage shows that the get_invoice_function returned the information that the invoice is paid.
  • The last row beginning with AIMessage again shows that the AI agent used that information and phrased a written response.

There was no need for us to program the execution logic. The AI agent was able to work this out on its own, based on the different information that was provided

  • The tools and their descriptions
  • The system message
  • The actual request

Let's make it more complicated and ask to send someone the status of an invoice by email. 

user_input = "Email Andreas the status of invoice 43" 
agent_outcome = graph.invoke({"messages": [("user", user_input)]})
print(agent_outcome['messages'][-1].to_json()['kwargs']['content'])

AI agent: I've sent an email to Andreas with the status of invoice 43. The invoice is currently unpaid but not due yet. The email has been sent to MASKED_EMAIL with the details of the invoice status.

MASKED_EMAIL does indeed mask a real valid email. Just protecting everyone's privacy. And indeed, this email was sent.

What were the detailed steps the AI agent went through for this?

[['HumanMessage', 'Email Andreas the status of invoice 43'],
['AIMessage', [{'name': 'get_invoice_status', 'args': {'invoice_id': '43'}, 'id': 'toolu_bdrk_013xPo3kEDLNujfEjnNc3vZ7', 'type': 'tool_call'}]],
['ToolMessage (get_invoice_status)', 'The status of invoice 43 is: Unpaid, not due yet.'],
['AIMessage', [{'name': 'get_email_address', 'args': {'name': 'Andreas'}, 'id': 'toolu_bdrk_013CsFB4ipU4FKtUuJpn9bDo', 'type': 'tool_call'}]],
['ToolMessage (get_email_address)', 'MASKED_EMAIL'],
['AIMessage', [{'name': 'send_email', 'args': {'email_address': 'MASKED_EMAIL', 'email_text': "Dear Andreas,\n\nI hope this email finds you well. I wanted to inform you about the status of invoice 43.\n\nThe current status of invoice 43 is: Unpaid, not due yet.\n\nIf you have any questions or need further information, please don't hesitate to ask.\n\nBest regards,\nSAP BTP AI Agent", 'recipient_name': 'Andreas'}, 'id': 'toolu_bdrk_011uCXBEjqCPHpvdX6oj7MWK', 'type': 'tool_call'}]],
['ToolMessage (send_email)', "I sent the email to Andreas (MASKED_EMAIL): \nDear Andreas,\n\nI hope this email finds you well. I wanted to inform you about the status of invoice 43.\n\nThe current status of invoice 43 is: Unpaid, not due yet.\n\nIf you have any questions or need further information, please don't hesitate to ask.\n\nBest regards,\nSAP BTP AI Agent"],
['AIMessage', "I've sent an email to Andreas with the status of invoice 43. The invoice is currently unpaid but not due yet. The email has been sent to MASKED_EMAIL with the details of the invoice status."]]

Here the agent determined the invoice status as before. Then realised that it needs an email address to then send the email. I think that's pretty cool!

Let's test with AI agent with another task. When asking the AI assistant, that is powering the answer_SAP_question() function, for the boss of SAP, the answer will point you to link on SAP's website. Now we want to go further and ask the AI agent the same question. 

user_input = "Who is the boss at SAP?" 
agent_outcome = graph.invoke({"messages": [("user", user_input)]})
print(agent_outcome['messages'][-1].to_json()['kwargs']['content'])

AI Agent: Based on the information from the SAP Executive Board page, the current CEO or boss of SAP is Christian Klein. He holds the position of Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of SAP SE. The Executive Board, which is the governing body of SAP SE, is led by Christian Klein as the CEO.

That's so much better. And it's not a fact that was trained into the Large Language Model. The log shows how the AI agent got the pointer to the website from the AI assistant (get_SAP_answer) and how it's used its skill to obtain text from a website.

[['HumanMessage', 'Who is the boss at SAP?'],
['AIMessage', [{'name': 'answer_SAP_question', 'args': {'text': 'Who is the current CEO or boss of SAP?'}, 'id': 'toolu_bdrk_01QLVPpJRP2UfeqgkL5vBdv8', 'type': 'tool_call'}]],
['ToolMessage (answer_SAP_question)', "To find the current CEO or boss of SAP, you should visit the SAP Executive Board page at https://www.sap.com/investors/en/governance/executive-board.html. This page will provide the most up-to-date information on the company's leadership."],
['AIMessage', [{'name': 'get_text_from_link', 'args': {'link': 'https://www.sap.com/investors/en/governance/executive-board.html'}, 'id': 'toolu_bdrk_01K1mJtaZvDwswZK1FWsTTix', 'type': 'tool_call'}]],
['ToolMessage (get_text_from_link)', "Skip to ContentContact UsService Request Investor RelationsCorporate GovernanceExecutive Board/Corporate GovernanceExecutive Board LeadershipSAP SE Executive BoardThe Executive Board is the governing body of SAP SE. Subject to the requirements of stock corporation law, the Executive Board is committed to SAP's interests and bound by SAP company policy. It provides the Supervisory Board with regular, prompt, and comprehensive reports about all essential issues of business, corporate strategy, and potential risks.Christian KleinChief Executive Officer (CEO)Learn more about Christian Klein\xa0Muhammad AlamSAP Product & EngineeringLearn more about Muhammad Alam\xa0Dominik AsamChief Financial OfficerLearn more about Dominik Asam\xa0Thomas SaueressigCustomer Services & DeliveryLearn more about Thomas Saueressig\xa0Sebastian SteinhaeuserChief Operating OfficerLearn more about Sebastian Steinhaeuser\xa0Gina Vargiu-BreuerChief People Officer and Labor DirectorLearn more about Gina Vargiu-Breuer\xa0Learn more about SAP SE Extended BoardLearn more about SAP SE Supervisory BoardQuick LinksSAP Trust CenterFind a solutionIndustriesFind a partnerTrials and demosFind servicesTrendingSAP SapphireSAP Business SuiteSAP Business Data CloudSAP Business AISustainabilityPartner ecosystemAbout SAPCompany InformationWorldwide DirectoryInvestor RelationsCareersNews and pressEventsCustomer StoriesNewsletterSite InformationPrivacyTerms of UseLegal DisclosureCopyrightTrademarkSitemapCookie statementDo not share/sell my personal informationBack to top"],
['AIMessage', 'Based on the information from the SAP Executive Board page, the current CEO or boss of SAP is Christian Klein. He holds the position of Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of SAP SE. The Executive Board, which is the governing body of SAP SE, is led by Christian Klein as the CEO.']]

 

Deploy the AI Agent as REST-API

Now deploy the AI assistant as REST-API so that it can be integrated into other applications. Here we deploy a REST-API on Cloud Foundry, but you can use any environment of your choice. In case you are new to Cloud Foundry, then Steps 1 and 2 of this Learning Journey get you started. The application that we deploy here consists of 4 files. The full code is available in this repository.  

  1. btpaiagent.py ➔ holds the actual application logic
  2. manifest.yaml ➔ contains for instance the memory requirements and environment variables
  3. requirements.txt ➔ lists the Python packages that will be installed
  4. runtime.txt ➔ specifies the Python version (python-3.12.x)

Before pushing the application to Cloud Foundry you need to make the following adjustments

  1. In btpaiagent.py specify your own SMTP server credentials to send emails. For productive use you may want to store these more securely, ie in the SAP Credential Store. An example is in this blog. 
  2. In manifest.yml enter your credentials for the SAP Generative AI Hub.

Then push your application to Cloud Foundry.

cf push

And you should see the URL of your REST-API!

 

Now an application can call that BTP AI agent. Let's test this from Python with a not so serious request. Let's use the AI agents functionality of getting the lunch menu and the TV program. Should I go for lunch or watch tv?

user_input = "Should I go for lunch or watch tv?"
backend_api = "REPLACEWITHYOURURL"
paylod = {'user_input': user_input}
headers = {'Accept' : 'application/json', 'Content-Type' : 'application/json'}
r = requests.get(backend_api, json=paylod, headers=headers, verify=False)
response = r.json()
btpaiagent_response = response['btpaiagent_response']
btpaiagent_response_log = response['btpaiagent_response_log']
print(btpaiagent_response)

And It's giving a good overview, especially when taking into account that both the lunch menu and the TV program were retrieved in German.

 

Put a sandbox user interface on top

To make all this more usable, here is a simple sandboxing user interface. For a productive project, you may want to use UI5 or SAP Build. This is also a Python application, now using the streamlit package for the user interface, deployed on Cloud Foundry. The full code is available in this repository.

  1. btpassistant.py ➔ holds the actual application logic and user interface
  2. manifest.yaml ➔ contains for instance the memory requirements and environment variables
  3. requirements.txt ➔ lists the Python packages that will be installed
  4. runtime.txt ➔ specifies the Python version (python-3.12.x)

Before pushing the application to Cloud Foundry you need to add the url of your own AI agent in btpassistant.py (the backend_api variable). Then push as usual to Cloud Foundry.

cf push

And this is the fruit of all the work. With a few basic tools the AI agent was able to let Peter know of the status of a specific invoice!

If you have fun with BTP AI agent concept, feel free to post here any tools / functions into the comments.

Or if you want to continue with another example, then @CanAbdulla has a hands-on tutorial for you to create an AI Agent with human-in-the-loop control.

Happy agenting!

entry.title: Where can I find learning materials on how to apply the machine learning workflow?
entry.content:
I recommend to access our Developing Classification Models with the Python Machine Learning Client for SAP HANA learning journey.

Overview
In this learning journey, you will explore how to apply the machine learning workflow. The steps from feature extraction to model evaluation are illustrated to ensure a comprehensive understanding of the process. The scenario presented in this learning journey focuses on preventing employee churn, a key business problem addressed using SAP HANA Cloud Machine Learning.

Learning objectives
By the end of this learning journey, you will be able to:

  • Configure the Python Machine Learning Client for SAP HANA (hana-ml) and connect it to SAP HANA Cloud.
  • Access and visually explore SAP HANA data using HANA DataFrames in Python.
  • Train a classification model for predicting employee churn rate using hana-ml Predictive Analysis Library (PAL) functions.
  • Perform model evaluation to assess the accuracy and effectiveness of the trained model.

Prerequisites

If you need some foundational information, you can also refer to
Developing AI Models with the Python Machine Learning Client for SAP HANA 

Please ask a question related to the digital learning Journey in the Q&A area. 

Our SAP Learning Experts will get back to you as soon as possible! We are here to support you. 

I appreciate your feedback and we will make sure to continue sharing interesting topics.

Kind regards
Margit

entry.title: The Rise of Agentic AI: A Deep-Dive in the World of Multi Agent Systems
entry.content:
Introduction

Agents are the talk of the town. As per the predictions of top enterprise companies, Agentic AI is the highest priority in 2025 and forward. Still in early stages of evolution, the capabilities in reasoning and collaboration are breaking records every day. Soon agents are expected to be more independent and handle complete complex business tasks without human intervention.

What does this mean for developers, consultants and architects - A HUGE OPPORTUNITY.

Developing reliable Agents, is important and more critical is to ensure the agents collaborate efficiently to complete the desired tasks.

In my blog, I am going to explain concept of Agentic AI and deep dive into Multi Agent Systems. The intent of this blog is to provide you the starting point and give you a leverage to explore possibilities on your own and ofcourse, with the help of your preferred co-pilot!

Along with the theory, my intention is to showcase a fully functional prototype which you can refer as well - GitHub Repo.

So, buckle up! and let's start 🚀

I will breakdown the article into following parts

  1. Introduction to Agentic AI and Multi Agent Systems
  2. Key components of Agentic AI
  3. Agentic AI flow diagram
  4. Solution Architecture
  5. Code walkthrough
  6. Lessons learnt

Introduction to Agentic AI and Multi Agent System

Without much theory, I will explain my take on:

What is Agentic AI?

  1. AI systems where agents operate independently, taking their decisions and executing tasks without much human intervention.

  2. The agents in Agentic AI framework use tools to enhance the outcomes.

  3. Tools can be APIs, LLMs or documents which can provide data to the agents.

What is Multi Agent System?

  1. Multi Agent System is an implementation and/or extension of Agentic AI.

  2. More than one agents work together on specific tasks.

  3. These agents can also collaborate and delegate the tasks among themselves eventually leading to the final task completion.

Key Components of Agentic AI

In this learning journey, I have chosen autogen as the choice of agentic framework for developing the multi agent system. This doesn't mean that autogen can be used in all agentic scenarios. There are lot of agentic AI frameworks available, each of it having their own pros and cons. I will provide few market leading available frameworks at the end of this blog for you to explore.

Autogen heavily relies on LLM orchestration to collaborate between agents.

Autogen provides a ConversableAgent interface with three types of implementations

  1. AssistantAgent - This agent performs the task
  2. UserProxyAgent - This agent acts as a human proxy and reduces human intervention
  3. GroupChatManager - This agent acts as a manager to manage between agents and delegate the tasks

Below diagram gives a abstracted view of the different personas of agents in Autogen. Reference - https://microsoft.github.io/autogen/0.2/docs/Use-Cases/agent_chat/

Figure 1

Agentic AI Flow Diagram

In this section, I have created 2 simple flow diagrams showcasing how the agents work and interact with each other.

UserProxy and Assistant Agents in Action

The below diagram shows:

  1. The UserProxyAgent delegates the task to AssistantAgent.
  2. The AssistantAgent uses tools which are functions to enrich their outcome based on data.
  3. The AssistantAgent terminates the request once the expected output is achieved and responds to the user proxy agent.
  4. The UserProxyAgent provides the final response to the user.

Figure 2

UserProxy, GroupChatManager and Assistant Agents in Action

In this flow diagram, I have taken a finance use case where we have two AssistantAgents

  1. Finance Agent - Takes care of finance related queries.
  2. CSR Agent - Takes care of customer communication.

The GroupChatManager agent is the orchestrator to get the job done.

The below diagram shows:

  1. The UserProxyAgent delegates the task to GroupChatManager agent.
  2. The GroupChatManager agent delegates the request to either finance AssistantAgent or CSRAssistantAgent or both agents based on the user requests.
  3. The AssistantAgent uses tools which are functions to enrich their outcome based on data.
  4. The GroupChatManager agent terminates the request once the expected output is achieved and responds to the UserProxyAgent.
  5. The UserProxyAgent provides the final response to the user.

Figure 3

 

Solution Architecture

This section outlines a high level solution architecture.

  • The  Agentic AI application is deployed on Cloud Foundry runtime.
  • AI Launchpad  is leveraged to provide a wide set of model library available to choose.
  • The generative-ai-hub-sdk is plugged in to integrate with the underlying model of choice.
  • Backend APIs can be securely integrated leveraging Destination Service.
  • The Agentic AI can be consumed by any Chat Client (Joule for example) of choice as it is being exposed as REST API.

Figure 4

 Code Walkthrough

As you get the high level architecture, now it's time to deep dive into the code. The README file will help you get started. I would like to mention some important files in this section for you to give extra attention when you are browsing through the code:

  1. server.py - The main starting point of your application.
  2. llm_config.py - Central place to store all LLM configurations for all agents.
  3. prompt_config.py - Central place to store all prompts for the agents.
  4. aicoreclient.py - Custom model for LLM if you are not using openai LLM. 
  5. weather_agent.py - Autogen weather agent showcasing UserProxyAgent and AssistantAgent.
  6. finance_group_chat_agent.py - Autogen finance agent showcasing - UserProxyAgent, GroupChatManager and AssistantAgent.

For weather agent, I have integrated with real weather API.

For finance agent, I have mocked the backend calls with sample JSON

I have shared a fragment of log below which shows the agents communicating with each other. Take a look.

user_proxy (to chat_manager):

Can you send a text message to the customer CUST001 as a reminder for his pending invoice INV1005

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
finance_agent (to chat_manager):

***** Suggested function call: extract_customer_id *****
Arguments: 
{"user_input":"Can you send a text message to the customer CUST001 as a reminder for his pending invoice INV1005"}
********************************************************

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

>>>>>>>> EXECUTING FUNCTION extract_customer_id...
user_proxy (to chat_manager):

***** Response from calling function (extract_customer_id) *****
CUST001
****************************************************************

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
finance_agent (to chat_manager):

***** Suggested function call: fetch_customer_details *****
Arguments: 
{"customer_id":"CUST001"}
***********************************************************

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

>>>>>>>> EXECUTING FUNCTION fetch_customer_details...
user_proxy (to chat_manager):

***** Response from calling function (fetch_customer_details) *****
{'customer_id': 'CUST001', 'address': '123 Business St, New York, NY', 'account_manager': 'Michael Johnson', 'account_status': 'Active', 'company': 'Acme Corp', 'email': 'john.doe@acmecorp.com', 'phone': '+1-555-1234-1234'}
*******************************************************************

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
finance_agent (to chat_manager):

I have retrieved the details for customer CUST001. The phone number is +1-555-1234-1234.

Please proceed to send a text message to remind them about the pending invoice INV1005.

Is there anything else I can assist you with today?

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
csr_agent (to chat_manager):

***** Suggested function call: send_text_message *****
Arguments: 
{"phone_number":"+1-555-1234-1234","message":"Dear Acme Corp team, this is a friendly reminder about your pending invoice INV1005. Please ensure it is paid at your earliest convenience. Thank you."}
******************************************************

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

>>>>>>>> EXECUTING FUNCTION send_text_message...
******Text Message****** : +1-555-1234-1234
###########################################
Message: Dear Acme Corp team, this is a friendly reminder about your pending invoice INV1005. Please ensure it is paid at your earliest convenience. Thank you.

Lessons Learnt

  1. Prompts – Prompts are very important to get the desired output. Write your prompts meticulously. Learn prompt engineering.
  2. Latency – Agents can go in loops at times and increase overall time for response and cost of the request. Agent evaluation is a key.
  3. API calls – Choose your APIs wisely and consider the response times. Reduce multiple API calls.
  4. Heterogenous integrations – APIs are available in different styles - REST, ODATA, SOAP etc. Integrating with different APIs can be a challenge.
  5. Focus – Keep your agents focussed on one particular task. If the task is going to take longer time then better to handle it asynchronously.

Available Agentic AI Frameworks

I have listed current few agentic AI frameworks available and are good starting point to start learning and developing your multi agent systems.

  1. LangGraph - https://www.langchain.com/langgraph 
  2. CrewAI - https://www.crewai.com
  3. Semantic Kernel - https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/semantic-kernel/ 

Conclusion

Drawing from my experience, I must emphasize that these insights can serve as a starting point, though their applicability may vary based on specific scenarios. This project will surely give you a holistic view from design and development of Agentic AI solutions and eventually deployment on cloud in production scenarios.

Take a deep breath and start coding  —  GitHub Repo

Stay curious! Keep learning!

Disclaimer: This is not an official reference application or documentation. The thoughts outlined in this blog are based on my experience and learnings about Agentic AI.

Feel free to “like“, “Share“, “Add a Comment” and to get more updates about my next blogs follow me!

References

  1. https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/project/autogen/ 
  2. https://microsoft.github.io/autogen/0.2/docs/tutorial/introduction
  3. https://microsoft.github.io/autogen/0.2/blog/2024/01/26/Custom-Models 
  4. https://github.com/microsoft/autogen/issues/2929

entry.title: New Machine Learning and AI features in SAP HANA Cloud 2025 Q1
entry.content:
With the SAP HANA Cloud 2025 Q1 release, several new embedded Machine Learning / AI functions have been released with the SAP HANA Cloud Predictive Analysis Library (PAL). Key new capabilities to be highlighted are introduction of text analysis, text vectorization, vector data as input to many more Machine Learning functions like classification and regression in the SAP HANA Cloud database. An enhancement summary is available in the What’s new document for SAP HANA Cloud database 2025.02 (QRC 1/2025).

Text extraction and text search enhancements

Text extraction from binary files

A new Document Filter Library (DFL) has been released, which provides functions to extract plain text from binary files like PDFs stored as BLOBs in SAP HANA Cloud tables. By use of the DFL_EXTRACT_TEXT_FROM_DOCUMENTS procedure, application developers and data scientist are enabled to unlock the valuable insights from text hidden in those files.

Extracted text is made available for text preprocessing tasks like chunking, NLP (e.g. entity extraction, sentiment detection), text vectorization using SAP HANA Cloud embedding models and further downstream analysis tasks based on text vectors such as similarity search, text mining, clustering and classification.

This enhancement not only widens the scope of your data analysis but also makes it easier to leverage text-based insights in your applications.

The Document Filter Library (DFL) is provided as a SAP HANA Cloud plugin. The setup instructions are documented in the SAP HANA Cloud administration guide and include the following steps

  • the plugin needs to be installed and Script Server activated
  • SQL user privileges need to be granted

Further note, the document filter library in SAP HANA Cloud provides comparable text from binary files extraction-capabilities like the text analysis and full text indexing from binary files features in SAP HANA Platform, for respective reference see the text analysis SQL API for extraction of text from binary documents in the Text Analysis Developer Guide or the SAP HANA search developer guide for document mime-type reference.

 

Keyword-based text search enhancements

Information retrieval from text typically starts with text search techniques. The newly released keyword-based search function BM25 search (SEARCH_DOCS_BY_KEYWORDS) has been further enhanced to support complete natural sentences as input to the BM25 search:

 

Classification, regression, time series and AutoML model enhancements

Hybrid Gradient Boosting Trees (HGBT) enhancements

HGBT now supports multi-target regression and multi-label classification models, alike MT_MLP modelling predictions for multiple columns using a single model.

  • Multiple DEPENDENT_VARIABLE values can be set as parameters to enable this
  • In addition, REAL_VECTOR columns are supported as target, and a multiple target regression model applied.
  • The objective functions supported for multi-target/label models
    • Regression OBJ_FUNC = 0 (Squared error)
    • Classification OBJ_FUNC = 7 (Softmax)
  • Using the new parameter USE_VEC_LEAF, allows to control for each tree leaf to have a vector-value as a predictor (default is no)

As the prediction output incl. values for multiple targets or classes, a new function  PAL_HGBT_MULTI_TASK_PREDICT is introduced generating the extended output

 

HGBT regression models with trend extrapolation for Time Series

Tree-based models like Hybrid Gradient Boosting Trees are great at capturing patterns from the training data. However, they falter when it comes to projecting these patterns into the future, hence especially when models like HGBT regressors are applied in time series forecasting. A regular such model has no means to infer trends or patterns beyond the bounds of the training data, making true extrapolation impossible. For a detailed discussion see for example this blog post: overcoming the limitations of tree-based models in time series forecasting.

HGBTs, now introduces a linear component to the tree-building process, allowing the model to capture linear patterns and thus trend extrapolation capabilities

  • New parameter MODEL_TREE, when setting it to linear, the model will try to fit a linear model at each leaf node.

With this key enhancement PAL Hybrid Gradient Boosting Tree (HGBT) now allows for building superior regression models, with improved trend extrapolation of value outside of the training data scope. 

 

Unified classification / regression enhancements

The modernized and much enhanced Multi-task MLP Neural Network modeling function, can now be used with the Unified Classification/Regression function

  • This unlocks local explainability when using Multi-task MLP models, the predictions providing reason codes with Shapley explanations (using parameters BACKGROUND_SIZE, BACKGROUND_SAMPLING_SEED)
  • However in the current update, the models are constrained a single target/label only when used via Unified Classification/Regression

 

AutoML for time series optimization enhancements

AutoML introduces an improved hyperparameter optimization for time series models

  • random-search optimization is now utilizing Hyperband
    • A new parameter WITH_HYPERBAND enables the enhancement in conjunction with SEARCH_METHOD set as "random"
    • The optimization now more efficiently allocates resources to different hyperparameter configurations, and significantly speeds up the optimization process

 

Python ML client (hana-ml) enhancements

The full list of new methods and enhancements with hana_ml 2.24  is summarized in the changelog for hana-ml 2.24 as part of the documentation. The key enhancements in this release include

Dataframe enhancements

  • New Vector- / vector-index management methods
  • New sort by vector-similarity

AutoML and pipeline modeling improvements

  • Data-parallel/massive AutoML
    progress monitor enhanced
  • New AutoML regression
    outlier detection

Text processing enhancements

  • New Text Analysis, POS, NER, Sentiment Analysis
  • Enhanced BM25 search by doc, sentence

Further misc. enhancements

  • New Tree Debriefing to text for decision tree
  • Enhanced CAP generation for AdditiveModelForecast, AutoML
    and Unified APIs wrt use of input/output signatures

You can find an examples notebook illustrating the highlighted feature enhancements here 25QRC01_2.24.ipynb. 

 

Generative AI-toolkit for SAP HANA Cloud (hana-ai)

Introduction

The new generative AI-toolkit for SAP HANA Cloud (hana-ai) is an extension of the existing Python ML client for SAP HANA (hana-ml), mainly focusing on generative AI-assisted machine learning scenario development using hana-ml, thus streamlining embedding of ML capabilities with SAP BTP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) apps.

It builds upon many leading-edge generative AI related open source Python libraries (e.g. langchain) and provides seamless integration with SAP HANA Cloud, HANA vector engine, and others Python libraries like SAP GenAI Hub SDK. 

Key capabilities for AI-assisted HANA ML development and code generation

  • Use code-template knowledge stores and new hana-ml tools
    • Tools for code generation as well as executable tools for agentic-use of HANA ML for Forecasting
  • Conversational agent for HANA ML
    • Generative AI agent, utilizing executable tools targeted for HANA ML
    • Unlocking agentic, AI-assisted HANA ML development
  • Further conversational interfaces and agents
    • HANA dataframe agent for invoke code template-based tasks using hana-ml in python
    • SQL agent for invoking code template-based and langchain-db tool tasks via SQL generation and execution
    • Conversational SmartDataFrame interface, for applying standard dataframe methods as well as code template-based tasks to a dataframe

Highlighted features

Using sample code knowledge stores

  • Leverage hana-ml samples as knowledge store
    • Python | SQL default samples for PAL functions
    • Can be augmented with additional content, created with custom / best practice templates

 

Using new set of agent-tools targeted for simplified use of HANA ML

  • Library of executable hana-ml tools
    • Tools execute independently and generate results, beyond code generation
    • Initial set of tools focused on Time Series Forecasting
    • Time series analysis tools applied to the time series data,
      determining guidance about which time series algorithms to apply
    • Can be augmented with additional, custom created tools
    • Utilized for automated and agentic-, generative AI-assisted
      HANA ML development or via direct tool-usage

 

Using new set of agent-tools targeted for simplified use of HANA ML

  • Library of executable hana-ml tools
    • Tools execute independently and generate results, beyond code generation

Using a conversational agent for HANA ML

  • New Agent for HANA ML
    • Ready to use agent to build HANA Cloud PAL forecast models
    • Conversational agent session incl. chat history, based on langchain
    • Leverages executable tools specifically targeted for HANA ML
    • HANA ML-tools: initially focused on Time Series Forecasting like
      automatic time series data analysis, forecast algorithm proposal
      and fitting, or CAP project artifact generation

 

The new generative AI-toolkit unlocks and simplifies embedded AI SAP BTP application development with natural language assistance providing a faster getting-started experience, automated code generation based on template code samples, hana-ml tools for an conversational agent.

For further details see the Introduction at github.com/SAP/generative-ai-toolkit-for-sap-hana-cloud and the documentation at sap.github.io/generative-ai-toolkit-for-sap-hana-cloud.

A sample python notebook using the conversational agent can be found at github.com/SAP-samples/hana-ml-samples/.../Generative-AI-toolkit-SAPHANACloud-Demo-SalesRefunds-Forecasting

Setup instruction for generative AI-toolkit (hana-ai)

  • Download the hana-ai package from github releases and install it using _pip install generative-ai-toolkit-for-sap-hana-cloud-x.x.x.zip_, or use _pip install hana-ai_ for install from pypi.org/hana-ai.

  • Setup of the SAP generative AI hub SDK
    • Install the required Python packages pip install "generative-ai-hub-sdk[all]".
    • Create a deployment for a generative AI model in the SAP BTP, for instructions see the SAP AI core documentation section Create a Deployment for a Generative AI Model.
    • The generative AI hub SDK reuses configuration settings from the AI-core-SDK, these include client ID, client secret, authentication URL, base URL, and resource group. You can set these values as environment variables or via a config file. For detailed instructions see this SAP Learning unit on using Generative-AI-Hub-SDK.

 Overall, again a great set of Machine Learning and AI capabilities enhancements with this SAP HANA Cloud release!

entry.title: 🇪🇸 Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core CodeJam
entry.content:
On the 30th of May, there will be an SAP CodeJam event on the topic of Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core and Python. You do not need to have any background in the topic, but a lot of curiosity!

RSVP Here -> https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam/getting-started-with-generative-ai-hub-on-sap-ai-core/ec-p/14082043#M820

In this CodeJam you will learn how to use Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core to implement a retrieval augmented generation (RAG) use case to improve the responses of large language models (LLMs) and reduce hallucinations. You will learn how to deploy an LLM and the orchestration service on SAP AI Core and query it via SAP AI Launchpad and the Python SDK. Furthermore, you will learn about the most important genAI concepts and create and use embeddings to improve your RAG response and build your own chatbot with memory.

This is an in-person event only (not virtual) and is planned for Friday, May 30th, 2025 at the SAP office in Madrid 🇪🇸 from 09:30 - 14:30 local time. The language of the event and the content will be English.

entry.title: Invoice Validation with SAP Document Information Extraction API & Python
entry.content:
Invoices are essential, but validating them manually can be tedious and error-prone.
